---
title: "2.2 Boolean Expressions"
subtitle: "Part 1: Equality, Relational Operators, and Remainder"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Testing Equality

### `==` and `!=` return Boolean values

The relational operators `==` and `!=` (not equal) can be used to compare values.

They return `true` or `false` <span class="term">boolean values</span> (布尔值).

Note from the textbook:

::: {.tight-list}
- one `=` sign changes the value of a variable
- two `==` equal signs test if a variable holds a certain value without changing it
:::

## Code Task

### `activecode:: bool1`

Textbook prompt: What will the code below print out? Try to guess before you run it. Then add 3 more lines of code that sets `year` to `15` and prints out whether `age` is equal to `year` and whether `age` is not equal to `year`.

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class BoolTest1
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int age = 15;
        int year = 14;
        // Will this print true or false?
        System.out.println(age == year);
        // Will this print true or false?
        System.out.println(age != year);

        // 1. Add code that sets year to 15
        // 2. Print out whether age equals year
        // 3. Print out whether age does not equal year
    }
}
```
:::

## Expected Output

### `BoolTest1`

The Runestone test expects this `main` output after the three added lines:

```text
false
true
true
false
```

The first two lines come from comparing `15` and `14`; the last two lines come after setting `year` to `15`.

## Object References

### `==` can compare references to objects

We can also use `==` or `!=` to test if two reference values, like `Turtle` and `String` objects, refer to the same object.

![Turtle Reference Equality](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-2-boolean-expressions/turtleEquality.png){fig-align="center" width="46%"}

If two reference variables refer to the same object like the turtle on the right in the image, the test with `==` will return `true`.

## Code Task

### `activecode:: boolRef`

Textbook prompt: Guess the output. Then add another `Turtle friend2` and set it to `juan`. Does `friend2 == juan`? Does `friend2 == friend`?

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.util.*;

public class BoolTestRef
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        World world = new World(300, 300);
        Turtle juan = new Turtle(world);
        Turtle mia = new Turtle(world);

        // Will these print true or false?
        System.out.println(juan == mia);
        Turtle friend = mia; // set friend to be an alias for mia
        System.out.println(friend == mia);
        // Create another Turtle friend2 and set it to juan
        // Does friend2 == juan?
        // Does friend2 == friend?
    }
}
```
:::

## Expected Output

### `BoolTestRef`

The Runestone test expects this `main` output after adding `friend2`:

```text
false
true
true
false
```

The result depends on whether two variables refer to the same object in memory.

## Relational Operators

### Numeric comparisons

The <span class="term">relational operators</span> below are used to compare numeric values or arithmetic expressions.

::: {.table-fit}
| operator | meaning |
|---|---|
| `<` | Less Than |
| `>` | Greater Than |
| `<=` | Less than or equal to |
| `>=` | Greater than or equal to |
| `==` | Equals |
| `!=` | Does not equal |
:::

Java uses `compareTo` and `equals` for comparing `String` values.

## Relational Operator Notes

### Remembering `<`, `>`, `<=`, and `>=`

Textbook notes:

::: {.tight-list}
- Think of `<` and `>` as arrows where the pointy end should point to the smaller value.
- A `<` expression is true if the smaller number is on the left.
- A `>` expression is true only if the smaller number is on the right hand side.
- Write `<=` and `>=` in the same order you say them in English: less than or equal to, greater than or equal to.
:::

## Code Task

### `activecode:: bool2`

Textbook prompt: Try to guess what the code below will print out before you run it. Then, set `year` to `15` and print whether `age` is less than or equal to `year`.

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class BoolTest2
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int age = 15;
        int year = 14;
        // Will these print true or false?
        System.out.println(age < year);
        System.out.println(age > year);
        // 1. Set year to 15
        // 2. Print whether age is less than or equal to year
    }
}
```
:::

## Expected Output

### `BoolTest2`

The Runestone tests require:

```text
false
true
true
```

They also check that the code contains `<=`.

## Boolean Expressions

### Positive and negative tests

<span class="term">Boolean</span> variables or expressions have `true` or `false` values.

Relational operators can compare two variables or compare a variable against a constant value or expression.

```java
// Test if a number is positive
(number > 0)
// Test if a number is negative
(number < 0)
```

## Match the Expression

### `dragndrop:: BooleanExps`

Textbook prompt: Drag the boolean expression from the left and drop it on what it is testing.

::: {.table-fit}
| Boolean expression | What it tests |
|---|---|
| `x > 0` | `x` is positive |
| `x == y` | `x` equals `y` |
| `x < 0` | `x` is negative |
| `x != y` | `x` does not equal `y` |
| `x < y` | `x` is less than `y` |
| `x > y` | `x` is greater than `y` |
:::
| `x >= y` | `x` is greater than or equal to `y` |

## Testing with Remainder

### `%` supports divisibility checks

The remainder operator `%` is useful in coding. These Boolean expressions can test whether a number is even, odd, or divisible by another number:

```java
// Test if a number is even by seeing if the remainder is 0 when divided by 2
(number % 2 == 0)
// Test if a number is odd by seeing if there is a remainder when divided by 2
(number % 2 != 0)
// Test if a number is a multiple of x
(number % x == 0)
```

## Code Task

### `activecode:: boolRem`

Textbook prompt: Try the expressions containing `%`. Then add another expression that tests to see if `age1` is divisible by `3`.

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class BoolRem
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int age1 = 15;
        int age2 = 16;

        System.out.println("Remainder of " + age1 + "/2 is " + (age1 % 2));
        System.out.println("Remainder of " + age2 + "/2 is " + (age2 % 2));
        System.out.println("Is " + age1 + " even? " + (age1 % 2 == 0));
        System.out.println("Is " + age2 + " even? " + (age2 % 2 == 0));

        // Add a line that prints out whether age1 is divisible by 3
    }
}
```
:::

## Test Requirements

### `BoolRem`

The Runestone tests expect the output to include:

```text
Remainder of 15/2 is 1
Remainder of 16/2 is 0
Is 15 even? false
Is 16 even? true
true
```

They also check for `age1 % 3 == 0`.

## Remainder Warning

### Odd numbers can be negative

Textbook warning: Java's `%` is a remainder operator and not a true mathematical modulo operator.

Do not check if a number is odd with:

```java
num % 2 == 1
```

## Remainder Warning

### Correct odd test

That expression works for positive odd values, but a negative odd number has remainder `-1`. Use:

```java
num % 2 != 0
```

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- distinguish assignment `=` from equality testing `==`
- predict the output of Boolean expressions involving `==` and `!=`
- explain that object reference equality checks whether variables refer to the same object
- use relational operators only for numeric comparisons in Java
- use `%` to test even, odd, and divisibility conditions
:::

## End

### Continue or return to the course outline

[Next: Prime Numbers POGIL](2-2-part-2-prime-numbers-pogil.html)  
[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
